title: Pyrrosia piloselloides
intro: Pyrrosia piloselloides, also known as Chinese rope fern or Chinese lace fern, is a species of fern in the family Polypodiaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Pyrrosia piloselloides include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional Chinese medicine, Pyrrosia piloselloides is often prepared as a decoction or an infusion, and it is also used in combination with other herbs to make preparations such as tea, capsules, and tinctures.
taxonomy: Pyrrosia piloselloides is a species of fern in the family Polypodiaceae, commonly known as the polypody ferns. It is a type of epiphytic or terrestrial fern, native to tropical and subtropical regions of the world. The classification of Pyrrosia piloselloides is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Division: Pteridophyta, Class: Polypodiopsida, Order: Polypodiales, Family: Polypodiaceae, Genus: Pyrrosia, Species: P. piloselloides. This fern is characterized by its hairy rachides and rhizomes, which are covered with fine hairs. The species is known for its ability to thrive in a variety of environments, from shaded forests to open rocky outcrops.
uses: Pyrrosia piloselloides has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate conditions such as fever and gastrointestinal issues. parts: Pyrrosia piloselloides is a species of fern, and its medicinal parts are primarily the rhizomes and leaves, which are used in traditional Chinese medicine. preparations: Pyrrosia piloselloides is a plant species that has been used in traditional medicine, particularly in China and other parts of Asia. side_effects: Pyrrosia piloselloides is a plant species that, while commonly used in traditional medicine, may cause side effects due to its toxicity. The plant's sap can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Furthermore, ingesting any part of the plant can lead to gastrointestinal problems, including nausea and vomiting. Prolonged exposure to the plant's sap can cause photosensitivity, resulting in skin darkening or blistering upon exposure to sunlight. In rare cases, excessive consumption of Pyrrosia piloselloides can lead to liver and kidney damage.
